Important historical building where George Washington took the oath as the first president of the United States in 1789. Currently, it houses a small museum dedicated to the country.

The Federal Hall National Memorial is both a memorial and museum that commemorates the site and the first meeting place of the First United States (U.S.) Congress and the first inauguration of the first U.S. President, George Washington. Owned and operated by the U.S. National Park Service, the building is located on Wall Street of lower Manhattan.

Long before the existence of the current building, there was an original structure that was built between 1699 and 1703 and it served as New York’s second city hall.

After the U.S. victory in the American Revolutionary War and its subsequent establishment as a nation, the original building served as the meeting place for the Congress of the Confederation, the country’s first national government under the Articles Of The Confederation. The Confederation governed the new nation from 1785 to 1789.

Once the United States Federal Government was established in 1789 under the U.S. Constitution, the original site would be used to host the first meeting of the First U.S. Congress and the inauguration of the nation’s first president, George Washington. The inauguration of President George Washington occurred on the balcony of the building on April 30, 1789. After taking the oath of office, Washington would deliver his First Inaugural Address to the U.S. Congress inside the Senate Chamber of the original building. His speech indicated the important responsibilities of the U.S Presidency and promoted national unity for the new nation. After 113 years of existence, the original building would get demolished in 1812.

The second building and the current structure was fully built in 1842 and it first served as the U.S. Customs House, whom collected taxes on imported within New York City. From 1862 to 1925, the building would be owned and occupied by the Subtreasury of the U.S. Treasury Department. After its use by other government entities from 1925 to the mid 1930’s, the Federal government announced its plans to demolish the building in 1939. 

Due to the successful activism and preservation efforts by a group called the Federal Hall Memorial Associates, the Federal Hall would be enshrined as a historic site and designated as the Federal Hall Memorial National Historic Site in May of 1939. The memorial opened on February 22nd, 1940, President Washington’s Birthday.

The Federal Hall Memorial National Historic Site is considered to be a primary example of Greek Revival architecture, and it was designed by architects, Ithiel Town & Alexander Jackson Davis. Both Town & Davis used Tuckahoe marble to construct the building and utilized the “Greek Revival” style for the building’s design to evoke the democratic ideals of Ancient Greece. The “Greek Revival” style is  evident in the building’s exterior facade, and the colonnade of Doric columns.

In May of 2026, I brought my wife and myself for a visit to Federal Hall and we very much enjoyed every moment of our time there!!!

We were both in awe of Federal Hall’s breathtaking Greek revival exterior and the majestic colonnade of the eight Doric Columns. When exploring the building’s interior, we were mesmerized by the “Rotunda” particularly the vaulted ceilings and its spectacular central dome. While the building’s exterior features Greek Revival Style and pays tribute to Ancient Greece, the Rotunda is modeled after the ancient Roman Pantheon and is meant to evoke the ideals of the ancient Roman Republic.

By virtue of the information exhibits displayed in the building, we learned that Federal Hall was the site chosen by the Colonial delegates to make clear their opposition against the British taxation without representation policy. We also learned that the Declaration Of Independence was read to the public on the steps of Federal Hall and that the First Congress of the newly formed Federal Government met to write and adopt the U.S Bill Of Rights. 

While it wasn’t present during our visit, the bible used by President Washington to swear his oath of office is kept at Federal Hall and normally available for viewing.

Due to its distinctive architectural features, pioneering design and the importance that it has played in many significant events throughout America’s history, Federal Hall is listed on the National Register Of Historic Places, is recognized as U.S. National Memorial and has been designated as U.S. Historic District-Contributing Property.

Both my and enjoyed our visit of Federal Hall and loved learning about the building’s significance in America’s rich and storied history. We highly recommend for everyone to visit this outstanding historic destination!!!

PROS
*  The famous historical building where George Washington was inaugurated
*  Old structure of 250 years
*  Museum and exhibits of authentic stuff from the event are displayed in the museum inside
*  Exactly across the street from the impressive iconic and well-known New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) building

CONS
*  There was construction and restoration- no entry
*  Not on most tourism agendas or itineraries for a visit to New York City
*  The original balcony used is not there
